perturbation.original_text: 1.2 Distributor's relationship to Airspan is that of an independent contractor, and nothing in this Agreement shall constitute Distributor as the agent or employee of Airspan. Distributor shall have no authority to accept any order or make any offer (except as herein stated), or execute any instrument or make any commitment on behalf of Airspan.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 Distributor's relationship to Airspan is that of an independent contractor, and nothing in this Agreement shall constitute Distributor as the agent or employee of Airspan. Distributor shall have the full authority to accept any order or make any offer (except as herein stated), or execute any instrument or make any commitment on behalf of Airspan.
perturbation.explanation: The modification grants the 'Distributor' full authority to act on behalf of 'Airspan', contradicting the initial statement that the distributor is an independent contractor with no such authority. This creates a contradiction concerning the distributor's power to act and bind Airspan, blurring the line between independent contractor and agent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Agency Law - Apparent Authority (Restatement (Third) of Agency § 2.03)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts agency law, specifically the concept of apparent authority. While initially stating the distributor has no authority, it then grants 'full authority.' This could create apparent authority, where a third party reasonably believes the distributor has the power to act on Airspan's behalf, even if Airspan did not intend to grant such authority. This is a direct legal contradiction. The first URL provides general information while the second analyses the apparent authority doctrine.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: apparent authority | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you apparent authority Apparent authority is the power of an agent to act on behalf of a principal , even though not expressly or impliedly granted. This power arises only if a third party reasonably infers, from the principal's conduct, that the principal granted such power to the agent. The idea of apparent authority protects third parties who would otherwise incur losses if the agent's signature did not bind the principal after reasonable observers thought that it would. Typically, if an agent has apparent authority, the agent's principal will be held liable for the actions of the agent which are within the scope of the apparent authority. Agency Law The doctrine of apparent authority comes up often in agency law . In American Soc'y of Mech. Eng'rs v. Hydrolevel, 456 U.S. 566 (1982) , the Supreme Court upheld apparent authority as a legitimate doctrine under agency law, holding , "Under general rules of agency law, principals are liable when their agents act with apparent authority . . . An agent who appears to have authority to make statements for his principal gives to his statements the weight of the principal's reputation” -- in this case, the weight of petitioner's acknowledged expertise in boiler safety. Power of Position The "power of position" refers to apparent authority that is created by appointing someone to a position which carries recognized duties (i.e. manager or treasurer). In this situation, there will be apparent authority to do the things which are regularly and typically entrusted and expected of someone with the position title. In New York, this principle was explicitly upheld in Pasquarella v. 1525 William St., LLC, 120 A.D.3d 982 (N.Y. App. Div. 2014) , when the New York Appellate Division held that the manager of a company has the apparent authority to bind the company to contracts , regardless of whether he has actual authority. Even if the principal has expressly placed limitations on the agent's abilities, but these limitations are not known, then the agent will still have the apparent authority to do those things. [Last reviewed in June of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords business law corporations AGENCY LAW Agency fiduciary duty FIDUCIARY LIABILITY fiduciary relationship Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law agency contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement and creates a situation where the distributor has 'full authority' to act on behalf of Airspan. This introduces the concept of apparent authority, as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The snippet defines apparent authority as 'the power of an agent to act on behalf of a principal, even though not expressly or impliedly granted,' arising when 'a third party reasonably infers, from the principal's conduct, that the principal granted such power to the agent.' By granting the distributor full authority, Airspan creates the appearance of agency, which contradicts the initial intention of an independent contractor relationship where no such authority exists. This misalignment of terminology creates a legal contradiction because the contract initially denies agency but then grants powers associated with it.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 All Equipment and Software will be delivered by Airspan CIP (as defined in Incoterms 2000) to an airport in South Dakota that completes international customs clearances and shipped to a location within the city limits of the above arrival airport, such airport and delivery location are to be agreed upon in writing by the parties for each Purchase Order. Airspan will be responsible for and pay all packing, shipping, freight, and insurance charges to the agreed upon location within the city limits.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 All Equipment and Software will be delivered by Airspan Ex Works (as defined in Incoterms 2000) from Airspan's factory. Distributor will be responsible for arranging and paying for all transportation, including export clearance, freight, and insurance from Airspan’s premises.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract states Airspan is responsible for shipping costs to South Dakota under CIP incoterm. The modified text changes this to Ex Works (EXW), which means the buyer (Distributor) is responsible for all costs and risks associated with taking the goods from the seller's (Airspan's) premises.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-319 (U.S. law, since the contract defaults to US law if jurisdiction is unclear).
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the contract's allocation of shipping responsibilities. By changing the Incoterm from CIP to Ex Works, the responsibility for freight and insurance shifts entirely to the Distributor.  This creates a direct conflict within the contract itself.  The UCC is only indirectly applicable as the contract defines the Incoterm, but the Incoterm definition itself creates the contradiction. This change alters fundamental obligations related to risk and cost allocation in shipping. The first URL links to the Cornell Law page on UCC § 2-319 and the second URL to a site that has the UCC's text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. (1)Unless otherwise agreed the term F.O.B. (which means "free on board") at a named place, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which (a) when the term is F.O.B. the place of shipment, the seller must at that place ship the goods in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-504 ) and bear the expense and risk of putting them into the possession of the carrier; or (b) when the term is F.O.B. the place of destination, the seller must at his own expense and risk transport the goods to that place and there tender delivery of them in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-503 ); (c) when under either (a) or (b) the term is also F.O.B. vessel, car or other vehicle, the seller must in addition at his own expense and risk load the goods on board. If the term is F.O.B. vessel the buyer must name the vessel and in an appropriate case the seller must comply with the provisions of this Article on the form of bill of lading (Section 2-323 ). (2) Unless otherwise agreed the term F.A.S. vessel (which means "free alongside") at a named port, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which the seller must (a) at his own expense and risk deliver the goods alongside the vessel in the manner usual in that port or on a dock designated and provided by the buyer ; and (b) obtain and tender a receipt for the goods in exchange for which the carrier is under a duty to issue a bill of lading. (3) Unless otherwise agreed in any case falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) or subsection (2) the buyer must seasonably give any needed instructions for making delivery, including when the term is F.A.S. or F.O.B. the loading berth of the vessel and in an appropriate case its name and sailing date. The seller may treat the
perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the original contract's clearly defined responsibilities for shipping costs. Originally, Airspan was responsible for shipping costs under CIP Incoterms. The changed text shifts this responsibility to the Distributor under Ex Works (EXW) Incoterms. The scraped law snippet 1, addressing F.O.B. terms, highlights the importance of clearly defining delivery terms and the associated expenses. While the snippet refers to F.O.B., the principle of clearly allocating responsibility for shipping costs is relevant. The shift from CIP to EXW represents a substantial alteration of the obligation, making the distributor responsible for costs that Airspan was originally obligated to cover, resulting in a clear legal contradiction within the contract's terms.

perturbation.original_text: 14.2 Subject to Sections 14.3 and 14.4, Airspan warrants that the Equipment sold to Distributor under this Agreement shall, under normal use and service, be free from defects in materials and faulty workmanship, and that the Software licensed to Distributor under this Agreement shall conform in all material respects to Airspan's published specifications therefor. The warranty period for any item of Equipment and related Software shall be twelve (12) months from the date of delivery of such Equipment and related Software to Distributor as set forth in Paragraph 8.1 (hereinafter, this period of time shall be referred to as the "Initial Warranty Period.")
perturbation.changed_text: 14.2 Subject to Sections 14.3 and 14.4, Airspan warrants that the Equipment sold to Distributor under this Agreement may, under specific use and service, sometimes be free from defects in materials and faulty workmanship, and that the Software licensed to Distributor under this Agreement might conform to Airspan's internal specifications therefor. The warranty period for any item of Equipment and related Software shall be zero (0) months from the date of delivery of such Equipment and related Software to Distributor as set forth in Paragraph 8.1 (hereinafter, this period of time shall be referred to as the "Initial Warranty Period.")
perturbation.explanation: The original warranty provides a 12-month guarantee against defects in materials and workmanship. The modified text makes the warranty uncertain ('may' instead of 'shall'), weakens the conformity standard ('might conform' and replaces 'published' with 'internal'), and eliminates the warranty period altogether (0 months).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties (U.S. law since contract jurisdiction defaults to US law)
perturbation.law_explanation: This violates UCC § 2-316 regarding the exclusion or modification of warranties. While the UCC allows for disclaimers, they must be conspicuous and unambiguous.  The changes weaken the warranty to the point of being illusory and the reduction of the warranty period to zero effectively disclaims any warranty without properly doing so under the UCC. The first URL links to the Cornell Law page on UCC § 2-316 and the second URL to a site that has the UCC's text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the warranty, potentially violating UCC § 2-316 regarding the exclusion or modification of warranties. The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a mandatory warranty, while the changed text uses 'may' and 'might,' making the warranty uncertain. Additionally, the warranty period is reduced to zero months, effectively disclaiming any warranty without proper disclaimer language as required by UCC § 2-316, which discusses how to exclude or modify warranties. Snippet 1 mentions that conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable.
